- But the question asks how many would be *incorrect as to their claims of statistical significance*, which could imply false positives (i.e., significant result despite no true effect). This requires assumptions about the false discovery rate, which is not directly calculable from p-value alone without additional context (e.g., prevalence of true effects, test power).  
- Therefore, a derivation must be made based on conventional critiques of p-values near 0.05, possibly invoking arguments from David Colquhoun or others suggesting that p ≈ 0.05 can correspond to false positive rates of at least 30% or higher depending on context.
